one_line_takeaway: SynthID-Text's Mean Score detector has a provable unimodal TPR-vs-layers relationship that a simple black-box 'layer inflation' attack (stacking an extra copied watermarking pass) exploits to collapse TPR from ~0.88 to ~0.13-0.16 at FPR=1%, whereas its Bayesian Score alternative is theoretically robust to this specific attack because its TPR is monotonically non-decreasing in layer count.
key_finding: Empirically, the layer inflation attack (10 extra layers) reduces TPR@FPR=1% to 0.15 (GPT-2B), 0.13 (Gemma-7B), and 0.16 (Mistral-7B) -- i.e., on Gemma-7B, 87% of previously-detected watermarked prompts are misclassified as unwatermarked after the attack. The paper reports pre-attack mean scores averaging 0.548 (above the calibrated detection threshold of 0.510 for FPR=1%) dropping to a post-attack average of 0.494 (below that same 0.510 threshold). The Mean Score's TPR trend on Gemma-7B rises from 0.04 at m=1 to a peak of 0.88 around m=28, then falls to about 0.01 (i.e., down to the FPR) at m=100, confirming the unimodality prediction (Corollaries 1-2); Bayesian Score's TPR instead rises monotonically and saturates (Corollaries 3-4), matching the theory. The Anderson-Darling test results support that the CLT/Gaussian assumption holds at the tested (moderate) text lengths. The paper also notes, citing SynthID-Text's own reported figures, that at very short texts (50 tokens) SynthID-Text's maximum TPR is only about 0.3 at FPR=1%, underscoring detection is inherently weak for short outputs regardless of attack.
core_contribution: The paper presents the first theoretical analysis of Google's SynthID-Text (Dathathri et al., Nature 2024), a production LLM generative watermarking system that uses Tournament Sampling. Using the Central Limit Theorem, the authors derive closed-form expressions for the expected value and variance of SynthID-Text's two score functions (Mean Score and Bayesian Score) as functions of the number of tournament layers m, and use these to derive expected TPR@FPR. Their theoretical findings: (1) under Mean Score, TPR@FPR is a unimodal function of tournament layers m -- it first rises then falls, eventually decaying to the FPR itself, for both Bernoulli(0.5) and Uniform(0,1) g-value distributions; (2) under Bayesian Score, TPR@FPR is monotonically non-decreasing in m and saturates once the layer-wise collision probability reaches 1; (3) Bernoulli(0.5) is provably the optimal Bernoulli(p) g-value distribution for maximizing TPR@FPR under Mean Score (Theorem 12). Building on finding (1), the authors design and empirically validate a black-box 'layer inflation attack' that removes the SynthID-Text watermark by appending N additional tournament layers (via a copied instance of the watermarked LLM) using an attacker-chosen g-value heuristic that favors low-frequency tokens, exploiting the unimodal TPR trend to drive detectability down.
threat_model: Black-box watermark-removal attacker with only query access to a SynthID-Text-watermarked LLM (no knowledge of the secret key, g-value distribution details, or model weights beyond querying it). The attacker feeds the same prompt 2N times to obtain 2N candidate 'winner' tokens from the base m-layer tournament, then runs an additional attacker-controlled N-layer tournament over these outputs using a heuristic attack g-value gˆ_ℓ(x_j) = -N^ℓ_{x_j} (negative observed frequency, approximated via arg-min frequency since true model probabilities are unknown) to select a final output token. This artificially inflates the effective number of tournament layers and, by the paper's own unimodality theorem, pushes the Mean Score's TPR back down toward the FPR, defeating detection while still producing fluent output from the underlying LLM.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) analysis is restricted to the non-distortionary setting of SynthID-Text (the setting matching Google's actual production configuration), not the distortionary variant; (2) they present a theoretical TPR@FPR comparison of Mean vs Bayesian scores but do not give a comprehensive theoretical comparison against other prior (non-SynthID) watermarking schemes; (3) they note SynthID-Text, like other LLM watermarks, still degrades moderately under adversarial paraphrasing attacks, and provable robustness guarantees against such attacks are left to future work; (4) the CLT-based analysis is explicitly stated to require moderate-to-long text lengths -- for short texts, the theoretical TPR may deviate from observed TPR, which the authors partially mitigate via the Anderson-Darling normality check but do not fully close; (5) the attack's g-value heuristic (arg-min observed frequency) is described as only an approximation of the ideal probability-ratio-minimizing attack, valid mainly when the base model's entropy is high.

datasets_used: ELI5 (long-form QA dataset, used for prompt generation, as in original SynthID-Text evaluation)
quantitative_results: Layer inflation attack (N=10 extra layers) TPR@FPR=1%: GPT-2B=0.15, Gemma-7B=0.13, Mistral-7B=0.16
Gemma-7B: 87% of watermarked prompts misclassified as unwatermarked post-attack (TPR=0.13)
Mean score before attack: average 0.548 (above 0.510 detection threshold at FPR=1%); after attack: average 0.494 (below 0.510 threshold)
Mean Score TPR trend on Gemma-7B: rises from 0.04 (m=1) to ~0.88 (m=28), then falls to ~1% TPR at m=100 layers

Reference SOTA figure (from SynthID-Text/Dathathri et al., not this paper's own new result): TPR=85% vs. SOTA 73% at FPR=1%, 1,500 watermarked + 10,000 unwatermarked 400-token texts, Gemma-7B, ELI5, 30 layers, Bernoulli(0.5), Bayesian score
Reference short-text figure (from SynthID-Text, cited not produced here): max TPR ~0.3 at FPR=1% when text length = 50 tokens
Empirical validation experiment settings: 1,000 texts x 100 tokens, FPR=1%, m=30 default tournament layers, temperature=1.0
baselines_compared: SynthID-Text's own published empirical results (Dathathri et al., Nature 2024) used as ground truth to validate this paper's derived theoretical TPR trend
Mean Score vs. Bayesian Score compared against each other within SynthID-Text (not against external watermarking schemes)
No external/competing watermarking method (e.g., KGW/green-red list, Gumbel-based) is empirically benchmarked in this paper; comparison is purely with SynthID-Text's internal score-function variants
